prior.title: NC DEQ Air Quality Division - Programs, Monitoring, and Compliance Resources
prior.summary: The North Carolina Division of Environmental Quality (DEQ) Air Quality Division provides comprehensive air quality management including statewide monitoring networks, emissions permitting, compliance enforcement, and public education. Key initiatives include vehicle emission inspections (I&M Program), Volkswagen Settlement investments ($92M for pollution reduction), fine particulate matter standards updates, and greenhouse gas inventory tracking through 2050.
prior.full_brief:
# NC DEQ Air Quality Division — Programs, Monitoring, and Compliance Resources

## Research Summary

---

# 1. What the Research Found

**Context.** This brief synthesises publicly available reporting from NC DEQ's Division of Air Quality (DAQ) across six official sources published between December 2025 and February 2026. It covers DAQ's ambient monitoring architecture, compliance infrastructure, greenhouse gas (GHG) inventory findings, and the forward trajectory of North Carolina's air quality regulation. The audience is operations and commercial leadership evaluating North Carolina as a freight operating environment, facility siting jurisdiction, or supply-chain footprint location.

**Ambient air quality — headline finding.** North Carolina has been in compliance with every federal health-based air quality standard since 2015, and as of 2024 average concentrations of criteria pollutants are well below each of the primary standards. *Source: NC DEQ, Air Quality Trends in North Carolina, December 2025. https://www.deq.nc.gov/about/divisions/air-quality/about-air-quality/air-quality-trends-north-carolina

**Criteria pollutant emission reductions, 1990–2022.** The 2025 Air Quality Trends report documents the following declines in statewide criteria pollutant emissions from 1990 through 2022: SO₂ down 95%; CO down 74%; NOx down 71%; PM2.5 down 48%; VOCs down 67%. *Source: NC DEQ Press Release, "NC air quality has vastly improved since '90s, DEQ report shows," December 19, 2025. https://www.deq.nc.gov/news/press-releases/2025/12/19/nc-air-quality-has-vastly-improved-90s-deq-report-shows

**On-road transport emissions.** From 1990 through 2022, CO, NOx, and VOC emissions from on-road sources declined by 81%, 72%, and 85%, respectively. The state expects to see further reductions from the transportation sector in the coming years due to the growing adoption of electric vehicles. *Source: NC DEQ, Air Quality Trends in North Carolina, December 2025. https://www.deq.nc.gov/about/divisions/air-quality/about-air-quality/air-quality-trends-north-carolina

**Hazardous and toxic air pollutants.** From 1993–2022, combined emissions of federally designated Hazardous Air Pollutants and state-designated Toxic Air Pollutants fell by more than 108 million pounds, an 82% drop. *Source: NC DEQ Press Release, December 19, 2025. https://www.deq.nc.gov/news/press-releases/2025/12/19/nc-air-quality-has-vastly-improved-90s-deq-report-shows

**Ozone exceedance days.** From 2015–2024, North Carolina recorded just two days of "Code Red" or above for ozone in the state, compared to 83 such days from 2005–2014. *Source: NC DEQ, Air Quality Trends in North Carolina, December 2025. https://www.deq.nc.gov/about/divisions/air-quality/about-air-quality/air-quality-trends-north-carolina

**Visibility.** In 2023, visitors could see as far as 119 miles at Great Smoky Mountains National Park during an average clear day, compared with just 54 miles in 1996. *Source: NC DEQ Press Release, December 19, 2025. https://www.deq.nc.gov/news/press-releases/2025/12/19/nc-air-quality-has-vastly-improved-90s-deq-report-shows

**GHG inventory — 2026 update (most recent).** The January 2026 GHG inventory update covers historical data from 1990 to 2022 and projects emissions through 2050. Between 2005 and 2022, North Carolina reduced gross greenhouse gas emissions by approximately 21% and net emissions (which account for all carbon absorbed by natural lands) by 30%. Emissions fell even as North Carolina's population grew 23% and real Gross State Product increased 38% during the same time. *Source: NC DEQ Press Release, "NC's greenhouse gas emissions lowest in decades, but temporary uptick projected," February 23, 2026. https://www.deq.nc.gov/news/press-releases/2026/02/23/ncs-greenhouse-gas-emissions-lowest-decades-temporary-uptick-projected

**Near-term GHG uptick.** Net emissions are projected to increase 7% between 2022–2030 before beginning to drop again through 2050. The increase is largely driven by new forecasts from Duke Energy showing an increase in burning natural gas and coal. *Source: NC DEQ Press Release, February 23, 2026. https://www.deq.nc.gov/news/press-releases/2026/02/23/ncs-greenhouse-gas-emissions-lowest-decades-temporary-uptick-projected

**Transportation sector dominance.** The largest contributor to the state's carbon footprint continues to be the transportation sector, which in 2022 accounted for 39% of the state's gross emissions. *Source: NC DEQ, Greenhouse Gas Inventory (2026 Update). https://www.deq.nc.gov/energy-climate/climate-change/greenhouse-gas-inventory

**Carbon sequestration offset.** Forests, natural lands and agricultural lands sequestered an estimated 32% of the state's gross emissions in 2022. *Source: NC DEQ, Greenhouse Gas Inventory (2026 Update). https://www.deq.nc.gov/energy-climate/climate-change/greenhouse-gas-inventory

**Long-range GHG projection.** By 2050, North Carolina is projected to see a 48% and 64% decrease in gross and net GHG emissions, respectively, relative to 2005 emissions. *Source: NC DEQ, Greenhouse Gas Inventory (2026 Update). https://www.deq.nc.gov/energy-climate/climate-change/greenhouse-gas-inventory

**Methane share.** Methane (CH₄) emissions accounted for approximately 12% of the total greenhouse gas emissions in 2020. *Source: NC DEQ, Air Quality Trends in North Carolina, December 2025. https://www.deq.nc.gov/about/divisions/air-quality/about-air-quality/air-quality-trends-north-carolina

**Monitoring infrastructure.** DAQ operates a statewide air quality monitoring network; the corroborating source identifies approximately 70 monitoring stations. DAQ also operates an urban air toxics (UAT) monitoring network in conjunction with the national program (National Air Toxics Trends Station, NATTS), originally proposed and designed by the U.S. EPA in 1999. *Source: NC DEQ, Air Quality Monitoring page. https://www.deq.nc.gov/about/divisions/air-quality/air-quality-monitoring

**AIR tool.** The North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality's Division of Air Quality (DAQ) has partnered with the North Carolina State Climate Office to develop a new air quality tool, the Ambient Information Reporter (AIR). The new AIR tool contains statewide weather and air quality observations about past, current, and forecast air quality events. *Source: NC DEQ, Air Quality Monitoring page. https://www.deq.nc.gov/about/divisions/air-quality/air-quality-monitoring

**Compliance infrastructure.** The DAQ compliance hub covers: Continuous Emissions Monitoring Systems (CEMS) installation and requirements; Compliance Monitoring Strategy (CMS) aligned with EPA policy; Annual Compliance Certification forms and procedures; Excess Emissions, Deviations, and Malfunction Determinations guidance; Compliance Assurance Monitoring (CAM) under CAM Rule; tiered enforcement policy; Chemical Accident Prevention Program under section 112(r) of the Clean Air Act; and Special Orders by Consent (SOCs) issued by the Environmental Management Commission (EMC). *Source: NC DEQ, Air Quality Compliance page. https://www.deq.nc.gov/about/divisions/air-quality/air-quality-compliance

**Vehicle emissions inspection requirement.** Emissions inspections are required for vehicles registered in 19 North Carolina counties. *Source: NC DEQ, Air Quality Division homepage. https://deq.nc.gov/about/divisions/air-quality

**PM2.5 standard tightening.** The EPA tightened the national ambient air quality standard for fine particulate matter in 2024. *Source: NC DEQ, Air Quality Division homepage. https://deq.nc.gov/about/divisions/air-quality

**Volkswagen Settlement.** North Carolina is investing its $92 million share of the national Volkswagen Settlement on projects to reduce pollution, including electric vehicles and infrastructure. *Source: NC DEQ, Air Quality Division homepage. https://deq.nc.gov/about/divisions/air-quality

**EPA federal vehicle GHG standard repeal.** This year's [2026] inventory update does not factor in potential emission increases from EPA's recent repeal of all greenhouse gas emissions standards for cars and trucks, announced Feb. 12. *Source: NC DEQ Press Release, February 23, 2026. https://www.deq.nc.gov/news/press-releases/2026/02/23/ncs-greenhouse-gas-emissions-lowest-decades-temporary-uptick-projected

**GHG inventory methodology note.** For this reason, DEQ cautions against directly comparing the findings in this updated inventory to those in DEQ's previous inventories. *Source: NC DEQ Press Release, February 23, 2026. https://www.deq.nc.gov/news/press-releases/2026/02/23/ncs-greenhouse-gas-emissions-lowest-decades-temporary-uptick-projected

---

# 2. Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

**Air-priority and road-priority freight operations — NC attainment status.** *Operational implication:* North Carolina's statewide attainment of all federal NAAQS since 2015 reduces the near-term risk of new air-quality-triggered routing restrictions or clean-air-zone enforcement for freight corridors operating within the state, compared with non-attainment jurisdictions. This is material for workspaces that route road freight through North Carolina or operate air cargo at NC-served airports.

**Transportation sector as the dominant emissions source.** *Analytical inference:* Because transportation accounts for 39% of North Carolina's gross GHG emissions as of 2022, this sector will be the primary focus of any future state-level mitigation policy. Freight operators with road-heavy NC footprints are positioned directly in that exposure window, even in the absence of current binding mandates.

**Near-term GHG uptick and Duke Energy forecast.** *Analytical inference:* The projected 7% net GHG increase from 2022–2030, driven by Duke Energy's forecast increase in natural gas and coal combustion, is relevant for workspaces with NC-based facilities whose Scope 2 electricity emissions are tied to Duke Energy grid power. Facilities drawing grid power in North Carolina may see higher grid emission factors in the 2025–2030 window before the projected post-2030 decline resumes.

**EPA vehicle GHG standard repeal — unquantified exposure.** *Analytical inference:* The 2026 inventory explicitly excludes the impact of EPA's February 12 repeal of federal GHG standards for cars and trucks. If the repeal materially slows on-road emissions reduction — the sector already responsible for 39% of gross state GHG — the long-range 2050 projections in DAQ's inventory may overstate declines. Workspaces using NC-based Scope 3 transport emissions figures in sustainability reporting should flag this as a data-quality caveat.

**PM2.5 standard tightening (2024).** *Analytical inference:* The EPA's 2024 tightening of the PM2.5 NAAQS standard is the regulatory event most likely to affect compliance burden on fixed NC facilities with emission permits. Facilities permitted on older PM2.5 limits should verify current permit headroom against the revised standard. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether any specific NC DAQ permit or facility is non-compliant with the revised 2024 PM2.5 NAAQS, or whether any reclassification is triggered, requires legal and permitting counsel review on a facility-specific basis.

**Vehicle inspection zone — 19-county scope.** *Operational implication:* Workspaces operating or registering road vehicles in any of the 19 counties subject to NC emissions inspection requirements face periodic compliance testing that affects fleet management and downtime planning. The 19-county scope is not further defined in the source blocks; the county list is publicly available from DAQ but was not included in the source content provided.

*Specific county list for the 19-county emissions inspection program not available from the source blocks provided as of June 2025; consult https://deq.nc.gov/about/divisions/air-quality for the current list.*

---

# 3. What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

**Scope 3 emissions reporting — transportation baseline.** *Operational implication:* Workspaces that report Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) or Category 9 (downstream transportation) emissions for North Carolina road movements now have a state-level GHG baseline (1990–2022 historical data; 2023–2050 projections) from an official DAQ source. This inventory can serve as a corroborating reference for Scope 3 reporting, but the methodology note above (DEQ cautions against direct comparison with prior inventories) means year-over-year trend claims drawn from prior inventory editions may need to be recalculated using the 2026 update's revised methodology.

**Volkswagen Settlement — NC freight and fleet capital.** *Analytical inference:* North Carolina's $92 million VW Settlement allocation directed to electric vehicles and infrastructure represents a concrete pool of public capital that could subsidise EV fleet transitions for operators with NC-based road freight fleets. Workspaces evaluating fleet electrification in North Carolina should assess whether their vehicle categories and geographic scope qualify for settlement-funded grants. *Legal Confirmation Required:* eligibility criteria for specific VW Settlement project grants require confirmation directly with DAQ's program administrators.

**CEMS and Annual Compliance Certification — fixed facilities.** *Operational implication:* Workspaces with fixed emission sources at NC facilities (generators, boilers, industrial equipment) operate under DAQ's CEMS and Annual Compliance Certification framework. The DAQ compliance hub provides forms and guidance; the tiered enforcement policy and Special Orders by Consent structure define escalation pathways if deviations occur.

**Chemical Accident Prevention — 112(r) applicability.** *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether any workspace facility handling hazardous materials in North Carolina is subject to DAQ's Chemical Accident Prevention Program under Clean Air Act section 112(r) requires a facility-specific legal and regulatory review.

**GHG inventory as planning baseline.** *Analytical inference:* The 2026 DAQ GHG inventory, covering 1990–2022 historical data and 2023–2050 projections, is the authoritative state-level baseline for North Carolina environmental planning and mitigation analysis. Workspaces developing internal climate commitments that reference North Carolina operations can anchor those commitments to this inventory, with the caveat that the near-term 2022–2030 period is projected to show a 7% net increase before resuming decline.

---

# 4. Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly say — NC attainment status:**
The workspace can accurately state that North Carolina has maintained full attainment of all federal NAAQS criteria pollutant standards since 2015, and that average concentrations as of 2024 are well below every primary standard. This is a sourced, citable DEQ finding.

**What the workspace can credibly say — emissions trajectory:**
The workspace can accurately cite the 95% SO₂ reduction, 74% CO reduction, 71% NOx reduction, and 48% PM2.5 reduction from 1990–2022, and the 82% decline in combined HAP/TAP emissions from 1993–2022. These are DAQ's own reported figures from the December 2025 Trends report.

**What the workspace can credibly say — GHG:**
The workspace can accurately reference the 21% gross / 30% net GHG reduction from 2005–2022, and the 39% transportation share of gross emissions in 2022.

**Pitfalls to avoid:**
- *Do not* state that North Carolina's GHG emissions will continue declining through 2030. The 2026 inventory projects a 7% net increase from 2022–2030 before the decline resumes. Claiming a continuous decline is inconsistent with the current inventory.
- *Do not* cite the prior inventory figures as directly comparable to the 2026 update figures. DEQ explicitly cautions against direct comparison due to revised methodology.
- *Do not* characterise the EPA vehicle GHG standard repeal's impact on North Carolina's trajectory — the 2026 inventory explicitly does not model this, and any claim about its effect is unsupported.
- *Do not* describe specific permit obligations, CEMS requirements, or 112(r) applicability as applying to any specific facility without facility-specific legal review.

**Questions to pose in a client conversation:**
- Does your NC facility hold a DAQ air permit, and when was it last reviewed against the 2024 revised PM2.5 NAAQS?
- Are any of your NC road vehicles registered in the 19-county emissions inspection zone?
- Does your Scope 3 reporting methodology account for the near-term projected uptick in NC grid emission factors through 2030?

---

# 5. What the Finding Does Not Resolve

*Does NOT resolve whether the 2024 EPA PM2.5 NAAQS tightening triggers any NC-specific non-attainment redesignation, new State Implementation Plan obligations, or facility-level permit revisions — see NC DAQ Planning and Permitting pages and EPA's official redesignation records for binding answers.*

*Specific [penalty schedule / civil penalty quantum] for DAQ enforcement actions not available from primary sources as of June 2025; the DAQ civil penalty assessments page lists past actions but the source blocks do not contain a penalty schedule.*

*Specific [county list for the 19-county vehicle emissions inspection program] not available from the source blocks as of June 2025.*

*The finding does not resolve the magnitude of the EPA vehicle GHG standard repeal's effect on North Carolina's on-road emission trajectory; the 2026 inventory explicitly excludes this variable.*

*The finding does not establish whether any specific freight operator facility in North Carolina is subject to the Chemical Accident Prevention Program under CAA section 112(r); that determination is facility-specific.*

---

| NC DEQ Air Quality Trends in North Carolina (2025 Update) | https://www.deq.nc.gov/about/divisions/air-quality/about-air-quality/air-quality-trends-north-carolina | 1 | Primary DEQ trends report page with quantified criteria pollutant emission reductions, ozone exceedance data, attainment status, and visibility metrics — the authoritative multi-decade emission trend record for NC. |
| NC DEQ Press Release – 2025 Air Quality Trends Report | https://www.deq.nc.gov/news/press-releases/2025/12/19/nc-air-quality-has-vastly-improved-90s-deq-report-shows | 2 | Official DEQ announcement of the December 2025 trends update; provides verbatim percentage figures for criteria pollutant reductions and on-road source declines used directly in this brief. |
| NC DEQ Greenhouse Gas Inventory (2026 Update Page) | https://www.deq.nc.gov/energy-climate/climate-change/greenhouse-gas-inventory | 1 | Official GHG inventory hub with 1990–2022 historical data, 2023–2050 projections, sector-level breakdown including LULUCF carbon sequestration (32%) — the authoritative baseline for NC GHG accounting. |
| NC DEQ Press Release – 2026 GHG Inventory | https://www.deq.nc.gov/news/press-releases/2026/02/23/ncs-greenhouse-gas-emissions-lowest-decades-temporary-uptick-projected | 2 | Most recent (Feb 2026) official GHG inventory release; flags 21% gross / 30% net reduction since 2005, 7% projected near-term uptick driven by Duke Energy forecast, and explicit carve-out for EPA vehicle GHG repeal impact — critical for forward-looking Scope 3 claims. |

---
